summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his Joel Test, a quick 12-step checklist for evaluating software team quality, positioning it as a practical alternative to the complex SEMA methodology. He notes it takes only 3 minutes compared to years of study. The post also mentions a Russian translation of his UI book and praises Windows 2000's automatic handling of international character encodings.
key_points: Existing software team evaluation methods like SEMA are too complex and time-consuming for practical use
Simple, fast heuristics for measuring software team quality can be more useful than rigorous but inaccessible methodologies
Unicode and modern internationalization support in Windows 2000 represented a meaningful leap in usability for multilingual content
money_quotes: It's a fairly esoteric system for measuring how good a software team is. It will take you about six years just to understand that stuff.
So I've come up with my own, highly irresponsible, sloppy test to rate the quality of a software team.
The great part about it is that it takes about 3 minutes. With all the time you save, you can go to medical school.
key_insight: A simple, fast heuristic for evaluating software teams is more practically valuable than a rigorous but inaccessible methodology.
